actions.thought: I am on the button with Ten-Six offsuit (Ts 6c) in a 3-handed game. While the button offers positional advantage, Ts 6c is a relatively weak starting hand that typically does not meet the requirements for a profitable open-raise in most standard opening ranges. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities in the early stages of this 150-hand tournament.

We are dealt 58o in the Small Blind. The Button folds. While we have the opportunity to steal the big blind, 58o is simply too weak to justify a raise, and limping out of position with this hand is a losing play. The standard and most profitable decision is to fold.

Playing 3-handed, the button opening range should be quite wide. Q6s is a decent suited hand with a high card that plays well in position. Opening with a min-raise or slightly larger is standard here to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I'll open-raise to 200.

8h 3c is an extremely weak, unsuited hand that has very poor playability, especially when forced to play out of position against a button open. Defending with such a marginal holding is mathematically negative expectation. The best strategic move to preserve my stack for better opportunities is to fold.

Qh2c is a very weak hand. Even though I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds to call the 100-chip raise from the Big Blind, this hand is difficult to play profitably out of position against a Button open. Q2o rarely flops well and is often dominated. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve the stack.

I am on the button with 7c-3s, which is a very weak hand with no playability. There is no reason to invest chips into the pot with this holding in an unraised pot. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

In a blind-vs-blind situation against a player labeled as 'Pro', 96 offsuit is too weak to call or raise with out of position. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better opportunities.

Holding Ace-Six offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a standard opening hand. I have the best position and a hand that is ahead of the random ranges of the blinds. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position.

I hold K4o in the small blind facing a button raise to 2.5 BB. This hand is weak, disconnected, and I would be playing it out of position against the pre-flop raiser. The standard and optimal play is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold pocket Jacks, a premium hand, in the Big Blind against a button raise. Flat calling allows the button to realize their equity cheaply, which is undesirable given the strength of my cards. I need to take the initiative, define my range, and build the pot while in position advantage relative to the button. A 3-bet to 850 is a strong, standard sizing to isolate the opponent and assert dominance.

Flashlight 3-bet from the big blind after my button open. While Ace-Six offsuit is a decent opening hand 3-handed, it faces significant disadvantage against a 3-betting range which typically consists of stronger Aces and pocket pairs. Continuing by calling or 4-betting would put a large portion of my stack at risk with a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop or when dominated. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I have 84 offsuit on the button, which is a bottom-tier, disconnected, and unsuited hand with terrible playability post-flop. There is no reason to put chips in the pot with this holding. Folding is the only correct play here to preserve my stack.

It is folded to me in the Small Blind with Ace-Six offsuit, which is a strong starting hand for heads-up play. I will open-raise to 300 to attempt to steal the blinds and maintain the initiative against the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Seven offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. This is a blind vs. blind situation where my hand is relatively strong. I am being asked to call 200 more into a total pot of 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. King-high is often ahead of a Small Blind's wide opening range, and while the hand isn't strong enough to 3-bet for value, it is certainly strong enough to defend and see a flop.

The flop is 7c-3h-Ks, which is relatively dry and favors my pre-flop opening range. Since I am the initial raiser, this is an ideal spot to make a continuation bet. My Ace-high hand has decent equity as an overcard, and a bet of 300 into a 600 pot should effectively pressure any middle-pair or under-pair the opponent might hold, forcing them to fold hands that have equity against me.

I have flopped top and middle two pair (Kings and Sevens) on a very dry board (7-3-K rainbow). This is an extremely strong hand in a blind-vs-blind battle. Flashlight's half-pot continuation bet is standard and could represent a King, a pocket pair, or a total bluff. While raising for value is an option, the board is very safe with no major draws, so calling is the superior play to keep Flashlight's bluffs in and induce another bet on the turn. Raising here might scare off weaker hands that I dominate.

The turn has brought the 3 of spades, pairing the board and reducing the likelihood of my opponent holding a three while solidifying the strength of anyone holding a 7 or a King. My Ace-high hand has marginal showdown value, but it is unlikely to be ahead if I continue to barrel against an opponent who has already called a pre-flop raise and a flop continuation bet. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free river card, rather than firing a second barrel into a likely calling range.

I have a strong two-pair with Kings and Sevens. On the turn, the board paired the Three, but my Kings and Sevens remain the superior hand over any one-pair King (which would be Kings and Threes). Since Flashlight checked after their flop continuation bet, I will bet 800 for value, as I am likely to be called by a wide range of weaker Kings or pocket pairs.

I am holding Ace-high with no pair on a paired board (7-3-K-3). My opponent's bet of 800 into a 1200 pot suggests they have a piece of the board, such as a king, a seven, or a pocket pair, all of which currently beat my Ace-high. Since I have no showdown value and my opponent is showing aggression, calling would be mathematically unprofitable and purely speculative. Therefore, folding is the best decision to preserve my stack.

King-Three offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even on the button. While I have position, playing such a weak hand out of position post-flop or even calling a raise from the blinds is generally negative expected value. Given that this is early in the tournament and I have a healthy stack, I will maintain a disciplined strategy and fold, waiting for a better opportunity to enter a pot with higher equity.

In a small blind versus big blind situation after the button has folded, Ace-Four offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately. It also balances my range for when I have stronger holdings in this position.

I hold 92o, which is one of the worst hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the small blind, this hand lacks the equity, playability, and implied odds to justify a call or a 3-bet. The mathematically and strategically sound decision is to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Holding Qs 5s on the button in a 3-handed game is a standard opening situation. Suited Queens have decent playability post-flop, and I want to exert pressure on the blinds to either take the pot immediately or play a pot in position. I will raise to 300.
With 8s 5d offsuit in the small blind facing a button raise, my hand has terrible equity and poor playability out of position. This is an easy fold pre-flop.

I am holding a weak offsuit hand (7c, 9s) in the big blind. Facing an open from the button, calling with 7-9 offsuit out of position is generally poor, as the hand lacks both strength and sufficient equity to continue profitably against a likely range. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic play at this early stage of the tournament.
Q9s is a very playable hand from the button in a 3-handed game. By opening with a raise to 250, I can often steal the blinds or take control of the hand going to the flop with position.

The hand Q7 offsuit is extremely weak, especially out of position in the small blind facing an open raise from the button. Playing this hand would require me to act first on all subsequent streets with a dominated hand range. Folding is the disciplined, standard play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In this 3-handed situation, I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x the big blind. After the Small Blind folds, I need to call 150 chips into a pot that will be 550 total. The pot odds are approximately 2.6:1, which is generous enough to defend a King-high hand against a standard Button opening range. K3o has decent equity against wide late-position ranges, and folding would be overly tight in this spot. Raising is unnecessary as my hand doesn't play well against a 4-bet range, so I will call to see a flop and utilize my position relative to the raiser post-flop.

On a board of 5d 4c 6s, I have King-high with very little connectivity. This board texture is generally better for my defending range than the Button's opening range, but K3o specifically hasn't improved much. As the player in the Big Blind, it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor here to see how they react. I will check and evaluate based on their betting action and sizing.

The flop of 5d-4c-6s connects heavily with the Big Blind's pre-flop calling range, which contains many low suited connectors, pocket pairs, and straight draws. With Q9s, I completely missed this flop and only have backdoor spades and overcards. C-betting this board is very low EV as we will often get called or check-raised by a stronger range. I'll check behind to take a free card and realize equity.

The King on the turn is a very favorable card for me, as I have now paired my King. Since Pro checked back on the flop, it is less likely they have a monster hand like a set or a straight. My top pair is likely the best hand right now, and I should bet for value and to protect against potential draws (like 7x or club draws). A bet of 300 into a 550 pot is a standard sizing to extract value from mid-pairs or drawing hands.
With Queen high and no pair or draw on a 5-4-6-K board, our hand has essentially zero equity against the opponent's betting range. Folding is the only sensible play here.

I am on the button with suited connectors Qd8d, which is a playable hand in late position. Given the stack depths and position, I will open-raise to 250 to attempt to take down the blinds or play the pot in position with the initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with Td 5h, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight opened with a raise to 250 from the Button. Continuing with this hand out of position against a raise is generally unprofitable, especially with the Big Blind still to act. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Defending the Big Blind with a suited King (K3s) against a Button open is a standard play. The pot odds justify calling 150 more chips to see a flop. The hand has decent playability and the potential to make strong hands like top pair or a flush, though caution is required post-flop due to the weak kicker.

I flopped a strong King-high flush draw on a paired board (Qc 8h Qh). As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking to the initial raiser is the standard approach. This allows me to see what Flashlight does. If they c-bet, I have plenty of equity to continue. If they check behind, I get a free card to try and hit my flush.

I hold a full house (Queens full of Eights) with Qd-8d on a board of Qc-Qh-8h. This is an extremely strong hand that is effectively the nuts, only losing to pocket Queens which are unlikely given my card holding. Since the opponent checked, I need to extract value. Betting 300 into the 550 pot is a standard sizing to keep the opponent interested if they have a piece of the board or a pair of 8s.

Facing a c-bet on a paired board, I have a King-high flush draw which provides plenty of equity to continue. The pot odds are favorable to try and hit the flush. A raise here could be risky given the paired board, as it might run into a Queen that won't fold, so calling is the most balanced and prudent approach.

With a King-high flush draw on a paired board, the standard line out of position is to check to the aggressor. By checking, I control the size of the pot and give Flashlight the opportunity to bluff or check behind. If they bet, I can evaluate the pot odds to see if continuing with my flush draw is profitable.

I have a very strong full house (Queens full of 8s). The board is Qc-Qh-8h-Jc. Since Pro checked again, I should continue to build the pot for value. A bet of 600 represents slightly more than half the pot, which is a solid sizing to get value from hands like a Jack, a pair of 8s, or straight draws (like 10-9), while keeping the betting manageable for a potential river value bet.

Facing a turn bet of 600, the pot is 1750. To call, I need to put in 600 to win a total pot of 2350, meaning I need about 25.5% equity to break even on direct pot odds. With a King-high flush draw, I have 9 outs, giving me approximately 19.5% equity to hit my flush on the river. Since my direct pot odds are worse than my equity, I must rely on implied odds to make up the difference. However, the board is paired and highly coordinated (Qc 8h Qh Jc), which introduces severe reverse implied odds. The opponent could easily already have a full house (like QJ or 88) or make one on the river, rendering my flush dead. Without the proper direct odds and with risky implied odds, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision.

In a 3-handed game, Jack-Nine offsuit (J9o) is a standard hand to open from the button. It has decent playability and allows me to take the initiative and play in position against the blinds. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) to build the pot or take it down pre-flop.

With pocket fours in the Small Blind facing a Button open, calling to set-mine is a standard and profitable play given the deep stack sizes. By calling, I keep the variance relatively low out of position while preserving the opportunity to win a big pot if I flop a set.

I am in the big blind with A7s. There is a raise from the button and a call from the small blind. I am getting 5:1 pot odds (150 to call to win a total pot of 750). This is a clear call with a suited Ace, which plays well in multi-way pots. Folding would be too tight, and raising would be unnecessarily risky given the two opponents and the fact that I can see a flop cheaply.

I set-mined pre-flop with pocket fours and missed my set. The 3c 9d Qd board has two overcards to my pair and features a diamond flush draw. Being out of position in a multi-way pot, betting here would be an overplay. Checking to see what the original raiser and Big Blind do is the standard play, with the intention of folding to aggression.

The flop contains no meaningful connection to my Ace-high hand, leaving me with weak showdown value. Given that this is a three-way pot, betting is high-variance and unlikely to succeed against two opponents who might have hit the Queen or Nine. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play to see the turn for free and keep the pot small.

I have middle pair (nines) with a Jack kicker on a board of Qd-9d-3c. In a three-way pot, checking back middle pair is often the best play for pot control and equity realization. Betting might force out worse hands but bloat the pot against Queens or draws that will put me in a difficult spot if they check-raise. By checking, I keep the pot manageable and can evaluate the turn based on my opponents' actions.

The flop checked through, but my pocket 4s remain a very marginal holding on this board (3c 9d Qd 2s). While I might currently be ahead of some unpaired high cards, betting here is unlikely to get better hands to fold and will only get called by hands that crush me (a 9, Q, or higher pocket pair). Checking to evaluate the action from Flashlight and Flash is the safest and standard play, with the expectation to fold if facing a bet.

The board is 3c-9d-Qd-2s, and I hold As-7s. The flop was checked around by all three players, indicating limited strength. With only Ace-high and no meaningful draws, I have very little equity to call a bet, but I also have no incentive to bluff into two opponents. Checking allows me to maintain a small pot and see the river for free, preserving my stack.

On the turn, the board remains relatively safe with the Deuce of Spades. Both opponents have checked twice (flop and turn), which suggests they likely do not have a Queen or are playing very cautiously. My middle pair of nines currently has significant showdown value. In a three-way pot, I prefer to check back here to maintain pot control and realize my equity. Betting might lead to a check-raise from a slow-played Queen or a big draw, which would force me to fold the best hand. By checking, I can see a river card for free and decide whether to value bet or call a small bet on the final street.

The river brings a King, making the board 3c 9d Qd 2s Kc. After the flop and turn checked completely through, my pocket 4s remain a very marginal hand, primarily just a bluff-catcher against missed draws. In a multi-way pot out of position, betting has no value because better hands will call and worse hands will fold. I will check and plan to fold to any action, hoping to take it to a cheap showdown.

I hold Ace-high on a board of 3c-9d-Qd-2s-Kc. The action has been checked to me on the river after everyone checked the flop and turn. My hand has very low showdown value and is not strong enough to value bet, as I would likely only be called by better hands like a King, Queen, or even a pair of Nines. Bluffing into two opponents after they have shown passivity is high-risk with little reward. Checking is the most prudent decision to reach a showdown cheaply or to minimize losses if my opponent bets.

The board has run out 3c-9d-Qd-2s-Kc. I have a pair of nines. Both the Small Blind (Pro) and Big Blind (Flashlight) have checked on the flop, turn, and river. This sequence strongly suggests that they do not have a Queen or a King. While the King on the river is an overcard, their repeated checking indicates very weak ranges, likely consisting of missed draws (like diamonds), lower pocket pairs, or weaker nines. By checking back, I realize my showdown equity with a high probability of winning the pot without risking a check-raise from a trap or a lucky rivered King. Betting for value is thin here as few worse hands will call.
8c 2s is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. As the first to act pre-flop from the Button in a 3-handed game, this is an easy fold. I will save my stack for a more playable hand.

I hold pocket aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With the button having folded, I am playing against the big blind. I will raise to build the pot and capitalize on my premium hand strength.

In this blind-vs-blind situation, Flashlight (SB) has raised to 350. I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Trey offsuit. I have position on Flashlight for the rest of the hand. Against a typical Small Blind opening range, Ace-high hands have significant equity and should rarely be folded. The pot odds are approximately 1.8-to-1 (250 to call into a pot of 450), which is more than enough to justify a call with Ad 3h. Raising (3-betting) is an option but calling allows me to play a pot in position and realize my equity without over-inflating the pot with a relatively weak Ace.

I am in the small blind with pocket aces. The flop is relatively safe (Qd, 5s, Th), providing me with a strong overpair. While there are some straight draw possibilities, betting now establishes initiative and protects my equity by forcing my opponent to pay if they want to continue with any drawing hands or second-pair combinations. A bet of roughly 55-60% of the pot (400 into 700) is a solid continuation bet sizing to extract value and maintain pressure.

The flop of Qd-5s-Th is not favorable for my Ace-Trey offsuit. Flashlight, who raised from the Small Blind pre-flop, has continued with a bet of 400 into a 700 pot. This board hits many hands in a standard SB opening range (Queens, Tens, and straight draws). With only Ace-high and no significant draws or backdoors, I have very little equity to continue. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Queen-three offsuit is a very weak starting hand with low playability. In a three-handed game, even on the button, there is no strategic incentive to enter the pot with such a poor range. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to contest a pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 4h. Since the Button folded, it's a blind vs. blind battle. Suited gappers like 64s are decent enough to open-raise with to put pressure on the Big Blind and try to win the pot immediately. If called, the hand has potential to improve on various boards. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative.

Opponent Flash is opening 3x from the Small Blind. Given their high activity level in previous hands, their raising range from the SB is likely quite wide. KTo is a perfectly reasonable hand to defend in the Big Blind because it has good high-card value, dominates weaker Kx and Tx hands, and we will have positional advantage post-flop. A 3-bet is an option, but calling keeps the pot manageable and allows us to realize equity in position against a wide range. I will call to see a flop.

I have top pair (fours) and a gutshot straight draw on a very draw-heavy, monotone flop. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I will lead with a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot. This bet aims to take the pot down immediately if the opponent missed the flop or has weak overcards without a club, while also providing some protection for my vulnerable pair. If I am raised, I will have to carefully re-evaluate based on the size, as the board is quite dangerous.

We hold King high with no club on a monotone and highly connected 4c 2c 3c board. Flash c-bet half pot. We have no flush draw, no straight draw, and practically zero equity or bluffing prospects on this wet board. Folding is the only viable option.

I am on the button with 9s 4s in a three-handed game. While this is a marginal hand, it is suited and provides an opportunity to steal the blinds from position. Both opponents have shown some willingness to fold to pre-flop pressure in previous hands, though Flashlight has been active. Raising to 300 (3x) is a standard opening size that allows me to take control of the pot early or see a flop with position if called.

K6o is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, we should fold from the Small Blind, as we are out of position and K6o does not have enough equity or playability to justify a call or a 3-bet bluff.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Eight suited, a solid holding for defense against a button open. With a stack of over 100 big blinds, I have plenty of room to play post-flop. The pot odds are favorable to call, and this hand has decent equity, especially in position if I were acting later, but even out of position, it is a standard defend against a standard raise size from the button.

I hold top pair with a weak kicker (Ace-Eight) on a board of 9c-7d-Ac. While I have a strong pair, the kicker is vulnerable, and I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flash). Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot controlled, gather information on the opponent's strength based on their reaction, and avoid inflating the pot unnecessarily with a marginal holding.

I have middle pair (nines) on an Ace-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor, this is a standard spot to continuation bet. The Ace is very much in my range, and a bet of roughly half the pot should be enough to fold out weaker hands like pocket pairs lower than nines, or seven-x hands, while maintaining the initiative. If I am called, I still have equity and position to evaluate on the turn.

I have top pair with an Ace-eight, which is a strong enough holding to continue against a standard continuation bet from the button. Folding would be too conservative given my position and hand strength. Raising is risky, as it would likely narrow the opponent's range to hands that beat me (such as a better Ace or two pair) and force out the bluffs that I currently dominate. Calling is the optimal play to keep the pot size manageable, gather information on the opponent's intent, and evaluate the situation on the turn.

I have trip Aces with an Eight kicker on a board of 9c-7d-Ac-Ad. This is a very strong hand, but being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most strategic move. It allows me to trap Flash, potentially inducing another continuation bet or a bluff, while keeping the pot controlled. Given the strength of my hand, I am well-positioned to check-call or check-raise depending on Flash's reaction to the check.

The turn brings a second Ace, pairing the board. I have a middle pair of Nines with a weak kicker. Flashlight's check on the turn suggests they might be holding a weaker hand like a Seven, a draw, or potentially trapping with an Ace. Given that I have showdown value and the board is now quite static, checking back is the best play for pot control. Betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm likely only called by better hands (Aces) or draws, and I'd prefer to see a river for free and evaluate Flashlight's action then.

I hold trip Aces (Ah-8h) on a 9c-7d-Ac-Ad-Td board. Flash checked behind on the turn, which strongly suggests they do not have an Ace, as they would likely bet an Ace for value. By checking the river, I can induce a bluff from missed draws or a weaker hand (like a 9 or 10) that Flash might try to represent as strength. Betting would likely fold out the hands I beat while only getting called by a better Ace kicker, so checking is the most profitable and defensive play.

I have showdown value with my pair of nines on an Ace-high paired board. Flashlight has checked every street, including the river, which suggests they likely do not have a strong Ace or they are playing extremely cautiously. The Ten on the river is a slightly bad card for me as it could have improved hands like JT or KT that Flashlight might have peeled with on the flop. However, my hand is still strong enough to win against missed draws (like clubs or 8-6) and potentially weaker pairs like a seven. Since there isn't much value in betting—as I'm unlikely to get called by worse and might get check-raised by better—checking back to realize my showdown value is the most logical play.

I am on the Button (first to act pre-flop in a 3-handed game) and dealt 73 offsuit. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em, possessing no high card value, suitedness, or connectedness. Raising as a steal with such a low-equity hand is unnecessary and calling is definitely losing in the long run. The mathematically optimal play is to easily fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded. With Ace-Eight offsuit, I have a significant equity advantage over a random big blind range. Raising allows me to take initiative in the pot and apply pressure. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to try and take the pot down pre-flop or set up a continuation bet if needed.

J3 offsuit is a very weak hand, even when facing a steal attempt from the small blind. Although I would have position post-flop, the hand doesn't play well and is easily dominated. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Holding 4d 8c is a very weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. Even though I am on the button, this hand has very low equity and poor playability. In the early stages of this tournament, it is disciplined to fold junk hands like this rather than wasting chips, especially when I have plenty of time to wait for a stronger starting hand.

With the button folding, I am in a blind-versus-blind situation. T8o is a reasonable hand to attempt a steal from the small blind. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300) to apply pressure on the big blind and try to take down the pot immediately.

Flash is opening to 3bb from the Small Blind, which he has done frequently, suggesting a wide steal range. I hold A6o in the Big Blind, which blocks strong Aces and has decent high-card equity against his wide range. Furthermore, I will have position on Flash post-flop. A call is standard here to defend the Big Blind and evaluate the flop in position.

I was the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-versus-blind situation. The flop of Qs 2s 5d is relatively dry, although it does contain a flush draw. As the Small Blind, I have the range advantage here, and a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) allows me to represent a Queen or a strong pocket pair. This bet size is designed to fold out the Big Blind's air and weaker hands that missed the flop completely. If called, I have a backdoor flush draw with the Ten of Spades, but my primary goal is to take the pot down immediately.

On a Qs 2s 5d flop, our hand Ac 6s is just Ace-high with a weak kicker and no redraws (no spades, no direct straight draws). Flash's half-pot continuation bet puts us in a spot where floating with marginal Ace-high out of position (wait, we are in position but still weak) is unprofitable, especially without any equity to improve. Folding is the standard, disciplined choice.

In a 3-handed game, opening from the button with any King is generally profitable as it has significant card removal and post-flop potential against wide defending ranges from the blinds. I have been active from the button in previous hands and will continue to exert pressure with a standard 3x open raise.

Qh6c is a weak offsuit hand, and I am out of position in the Small Blind. Even against a potentially loose button open from Flash, this hand doesn't have enough playability or equity to call or 3-bet. Folding is the standard, most profitable choice.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Trey suited, a strong enough hand to defend against a button open. With 200 chips to call to win a 450 pot, I am getting good odds, and the suited nature of the hand provides decent post-flop playability. Calling is the standard, disciplined play here to see a flop against an active opponent.

I hold Ace-Trey on a 7-9-5 board. I have missed the flop entirely, holding only Ace-high with no significant draws. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the most standard and disciplined play is to check, allowing me to evaluate the opponent's action or potential turn card without committing more chips to a weak hand.

The board texture (7-9-5 with two clubs) is quite coordinated and favors the Big Blind's defending range, which contains many middling connectors and pairs. While I have been aggressive in previous hands, King-high has some showdown value in a 3-handed game, and betting into a board that hits the defender so well often results in being called or raised by hands that have me crushed. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and hope to realize my equity if a King or a running straight card appears, or potentially reach showdown cheaply if the action remains passive.

I am in the big blind with Ace-high on a board of 7c 9c 5s 7h. The opponent checked behind on the flop, which suggests their range is likely capped or they have a marginal hand. With no pair and no realistic flush draw, betting here would be a pure bluff that likely won't force a better hand to fold. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a showdown cheaply or evaluate their action on the river, minimizing potential losses against a range that could include better hands.

The turn 7h pairs the board, which likely doesn't change the equity much between my King-high and Flashlight's range. Since Flashlight checked again, they are either weak or trapping with a 7 or 9. King-high has reasonable showdown value in a short-handed game, and by checking back, I control the pot size and ensure I see the river for free. Betting into this coordinated board risks getting check-raised or called by better hands, so checking is the most prudent play to maximize my stack's stability.

I am holding Ace-high on a board of 7c-9c-5s-7h-6d. The opponent has checked the river, and my hand has very little showdown value. Betting would only induce calls from better hands and folds from worse hands (which likely already lose to my Ace-high). Therefore, checking is the optimal play to minimize losses and potentially reach a showdown.

On the turn, the board now shows three hearts (Qh-5h-Jh). I hold the 7 of hearts, giving me a very weak flush draw if a fourth heart appears, but currently, I have no made hand and only 9-high. In a limped blind-vs-blind pot, there is no reason to inflate the pot with a hand that has almost no showdown value and minimal equity. I will check to see if 'Pro' also checks, which would allow me to see the river for free. If they bet, I will fold as my hand is too weak to continue against aggression on this coordinated board.

Flash has checked both the flop and the turn after limping in from the small blind, indicating a very weak range. I hold the 9 of hearts on a three-heart board, giving me a moderate flush draw, a gutshot to a King-high straight, and an overcard. Betting half-pot here is an excellent semi-bluff to fold out better high cards like Ace-high or small pocket pairs, while still retaining some equity if called.

I have 9-high and a weak 7-high flush draw on a board with three hearts. Pro bet 100 into a 200 pot after checking the flop. My pot odds (4:1 or 25%) are slightly worse than my equity to hit a flush (approximately 19.6%). Crucially, even if a heart comes, a 7-high flush is extremely vulnerable to any higher heart in the opponent's range. Given that I have no showdown value and poor implied odds, there is no reason to continue in this small limped pot.

I am on the button with pocket fives, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack and have been active with opening raises. I will follow my established pattern of raising to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop or set mine if called.

AJo is a strong hand against a Button open, but plays worse multi-way and out of position if I just call. 3-betting to 1000 puts pressure on Flash's wide opening range, forces out the Big Blind, and allows me to take down the pot pre-flop or enter the flop with the betting lead.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (5d, 4c). There is a significant 3-bet from the Small Blind (Pro) after an opening raise from the Button (Flash). Calling here would be a mistake as the hand has very poor equity, and investing 900 chips into a 3-bet pot with this holding is mathematically unsound.

I have pocket fives on the button and opened to 300. Pro, in the small blind, has 3-bet to 1000. With a stack of over 11,000 and Pro having around 7,300, I have sufficient implied odds to call and 'set mine'. I am in position, and the cost to call is 700 more into a pot that will be 2,100, giving me 3:1 direct odds. If I hit a set on the flop, I can potentially stack Pro. Folding is too tight, and 4-betting is risky against a 3-bet from the blinds which often signifies strength. Calling allows me to evaluate the flop in position.

The board of Ts As Qc is extremely wet and heavily connects with Flash's 3-bet calling range, which includes many two pairs, sets, and strong broadway draws. Out of position with top pair and a gutshot, checking for pot control is the safest line. Betting would likely isolate us against better hands or strong draws while folding out hands we beat.

I am in position with pocket fives on a very coordinated flop (As-Ts-Qc) that heavily favors a 3-betting range. After Pro checked, betting here as a bluff is unlikely to be successful given how many hands in their range (Aces, Queens, Kings, and broadway draws) connected with this board. My hand has very little showdown value and almost no equity against a calling range. By checking back, I take a free card to potentially hit one of the two remaining fives or simply move to the turn without bloating the pot with a weak hand.

The board pairing the Ten on the turn doesn't change much, though it counterfeits some two-pair combinations and makes trips possible if Flash floated with a Ten. However, it's more likely he checked behind on the flop with a marginal made hand like a weaker Ace, a Queen, or a draw (like Kings or a flush draw). Betting here might only get called by better hands (AQ, AK, AT) and fold out his bluffs or weaker holdings. Checking again allows me to control the size of the pot, keep his drawing hands and bluffs in his range, and evaluate his turn action.

The board (As-Qc-Ts-Th) is extremely coordinated and favors a 3-betting range significantly. My pocket fives have almost no equity here and very little showdown value. Pro, having 3-bet pre-flop and checked both the flop and the turn, might be trapping with a strong hand (like an Ace, a Ten, or a full house) or holding a hand like KK/JJ with showdown value. Since my hand cannot realistically improve to win the pot and it's difficult to represent a hand that forces better hands to fold on this texture, checking back to see a free river is the safest and most logical play.

The river King completes a Broadway straight for my hand (T-J-Q-K-A). Given Flash checked the flop and turn, it's highly unlikely he holds a premium hand that made a full house (like AA, QQ, TT, or AT) as he would likely have bet those earlier for value or protection. His range consists heavily of one-pair or two-pair hands like AK, AQ, AJ, KQ, or weaker Aces that will often call a reasonably sized river bet. I need to bet for value here to extract chips from those holdings.

The final board is As-Kh-Qc-Ts-Th. My pocket fives (5h 5s) do not play, and my best 5-card hand is simply the board: Ace, King, Queen, Ten, Ten. This hand is incredibly weak given the action. Pro 3-bet pre-flop, which places a high frequency of Broadway cards (A, K, Q, J, T) and high pocket pairs (AA-JJ) in their range. Any Jack in their hand makes a straight, any Ten makes Trips or a Full House, and any Ace, King, or Queen makes a Full House or a superior Two Pair (AA-KK-Q or similar). Since my hand only plays the board, I lose to any hand in Pro's 3-betting range that contains a Broadway card or a pocket pair higher than tens. Given that Pro is betting 800 into the pot on the river, they are likely either betting for value with a hand that beats the board or potentially bluffing, but even their bluffs (like small pocket pairs or lower cards) would only result in a split pot at best. Calling 800 here with only the board is extremely high risk for zero potential win and a low probability of a split.
9h 6s is a weak, offsuit hand with very little playability. Even on the button, it is not worth entering the pot. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

With the button folding, I am in a small blind vs. big blind scenario. Holding Jack-seven of diamonds (Jd 7d), I have a suited hand with decent connectivity. Given the pot odds and the fact that I am in position post-flop, completing the big blind is a standard and low-risk play to see a flop. I will call to complete the blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. Flashlight (Small Blind) has limped in. Based on previous hands, Flashlight tends to raise with their stronger holdings and limp with more speculative ones. Ace-Eight is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation, and I have positional advantage for the remainder of the hand. Raising here allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or build value against a weaker range. I will raise to 350.

I am in the Small Blind and facing a raise to 350 from the Big Blind (Flash). My holding of Jack-seven suited (Jd 7d) is marginal and performs poorly out of position. Calling 300 more to see a flop is not profitable given the lack of positional advantage and the strength of the raise. Therefore, I will fold to preserve my stack.

2c-8d is a very weak starting hand with poor equity and board coverage. In a three-handed game, this hand does not justify an open-raise from the button as it performs poorly against calling ranges. I will fold and conserve my stack for a more profitable spot.

I hold King-Ten offsuit in the Small Blind. In a blind-versus-blind situation, this is a very strong hand. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the Big Blind, which is a standard play to leverage my positional advantage post-flop or win the blinds immediately.

Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, Queen-Jack suited is a very strong and playable hand. I have position on the pre-flop raiser and plenty of stack depth. Calling is standard here to keep their range wide and play a pot in position where this hand can hit many strong draws and top pair hands.

As the pre-flop aggressor on a very dry board (J-3-4 rainbow), I am in a prime position to continuation bet. This board is unlikely to have hit the Big Blind's calling range hard, and I still have two overcards (King and Ten) as potential outs. A standard bet of half the pot should be enough to fold out most of the opponent's air and speculative hands that missed this flop.

I have flopped top pair with a strong kicker (Qh Jh) on a dry board (J-3-4 rainbow). Flash has continuation-bet half the pot. Given the dry board, my hand is well ahead of Flash's c-betting range, which includes many air hands, worse pairs, and backdoors. Raising here would likely fold out worse hands and isolate me against stronger hands like AJ, KJ, or overpairs. Calling allows me to keep Flash's bluffs and weaker hands in the pot while I maintain positional advantage going to the turn.

On this dry board of Jack-high, my continuation bet on the flop was called by the Big Blind, suggesting they likely have a piece of the board, such as a Jack or a medium pocket pair. The turn 7 of clubs doesn't significantly change the board state. Since I only hold King-high with no made hand and limited equity to improve, a second barrel is unlikely to fold out a Jack. I will check to see if I can realize my equity for free or evaluate the strength of my opponent's hand based on their action.

On the turn, the board introduces a flush draw, and my opponent checks. This check often indicates weakness or a marginal made hand seeking pot control, such as a smaller pair or a weaker Jack. I have top pair with a strong kicker, so betting for value and protection is the best play. A half-pot bet charges any draws, gets value from worse made hands like pocket tens or nines, and takes control of the hand in position.

On the turn, after I checked, my opponent 'Pro' bet 600 into a 1200 pot. I only hold King-high. Given that Pro called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, it is highly likely they have at least a pair (probably a Jack or a medium pocket pair) or a strong draw. My King and Ten are overcards to the 3, 4, and 7, but I am behind any Jack-x. I do not have the pot odds to call with only 6 potential outs (three Kings and three Tens) which might not even be good if Pro has a hand like AJ or KJ. Continuing here would be a low-equity play, so I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Queen-Seven offsuit. While it is tempting to open many hands from the button in a three-handed game, Q7o is quite marginal and doesn't play well post-flop, especially against opponents like Pro and Flashlight who have shown aggression and a willingness to defend their blinds. Given the current tournament stage and my stack size, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a better opening opportunity rather than risking chips with a weak holding that is easily dominated.

With 75s in the small blind and the button folding, I have a decent suited one-gapper that can easily steal the big blind or play well post-flop. Raising to 3 BBs (300) puts pressure on the BB and gives me the betting lead.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 6s. This is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Even though the price is decent to call (200 to win 400), playing this hand out of position against a raise from the Small Blind is negative expected value. It is best to fold and wait for a more advantageous hand.

With J9s on the Button in a 3-handed game, I have a solid playable hand that can make straights and flushes. Opening with a raise is standard to try and steal the blinds, or at least play in position post-flop if called. I'll raise to 250.

I am in the small blind with T3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand to play. Calling the raise from the button would put me in a disadvantaged position for the rest of the hand without a strong holding. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am holding pocket Queens, which is a premium hand. The Button opened with a raise to 250, and since I am in the Big Blind, I should 3-bet for value. A raise to 800 will put pressure on the Button's opening range and build the pot with my strong equity.

I opened with J9s on the Button, which is a standard steal/playable hand, but Flash in the Big Blind 3-bet to 800. Facing a large 3-bet, J9s is often dominated by their 3-betting range (hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, or high pocket pairs). The math and equity do not justify calling 550 more chips here, and 4-bet bluffing is unnecessarily risky. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play.

I am in late position (the button) with a strong premium hand, Ace-Queen offsuit. Raising is the optimal move to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds, or build a pot where I will have a positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with K7o. Flashlight has opened for 300 from the Button. While King-high has some value, K7o is a marginal hand to play out of position against a raise. In a 3-handed game, ranges are wider, but calling here would put me in a difficult spot for the rest of the hand, especially with the Big Blind (Pro) still left to act. Given my current stack size and the desire to wait for better spots, I will fold and look for more favorable opportunities.

Queen-Ten suited is a standard defense from the Big Blind against a Button open. We are getting good pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 450), and the hand has excellent post-flop playability with strong potential to make straights, flushes, or strong pairs. Calling is the best decision to see a flop.

On a King-high flop, I have middle pair with a decent kicker. Out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser and evaluate their action. This board hits the Button's range well, so checking is the safest and most balanced approach.

I have top pair with Queens and an Ace kicker on a board featuring a King, a Queen, and two diamonds. The King is an overcard, but I still have a strong hand. Since my opponent checked to me as the pre-flop raiser, betting is the standard move to protect my hand against potential draws like a flush or a straight draw, while extracting value from weaker hands. A bet of 300, which is slightly less than half the pot, is an appropriate sizing to define the situation and maintain control of the hand.

With middle pair on a board that hits the opponent's range, we face a standard half-pot continuation bet. Getting good pot odds (around 3:1), our hand is too strong to fold to a single bet since the opponent's c-betting range will include many flush draws, gutshots, and pure bluffs. Calling is the optimal play to defend our Big Blind range, and we will evaluate the turn card based on further action.

On the turn, the board pairs the bottom card and brings a second flush draw. Since I am out of position with middle pair, my standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) here doesn't accomplish much, as better hands will call or raise, and worse hands will fold. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate my opponent's action. If they check behind, I get to see a free river.

The board has paired on the turn with the six of clubs, significantly increasing the likelihood that my opponent holds a six for trips or potentially a full house if they are playing a suited connector or lower pair. While I have top pair with Queens, my kicker (Ace) does not improve my standing against a King or a six. Checking behind is the most prudent strategic move here to control the pot size, mitigate risk against a potential trap, and reach a showdown affordably, as a bet would likely only be called by hands that are currently beating me.

On a blank river where all draws missed, my middle pair is primarily a bluff-catcher. The opponent's turn check indicates weakness, likely a busted draw or a weak made hand. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands, but checking allows the opponent to bluff their missed flush or straight draws. Checking also keeps the pot small in case the opponent checked back a stronger hand like a weak King or better Queen.

I hold a pair of Queens (Qh, Qc on the board is not paired, but the board has a pair of sixes). Given the board state (Kd, 6d, Qc, 6c, 2s), a King or any six clearly beats my pair of Queens. The opponent checking to me on the river could be a trap or a sign of a missed draw. Since my hand has marginal showdown value and is unlikely to be called by worse hands, checking behind is the most disciplined play to avoid being check-raised or losing more chips to a hand that has me beat.

I have pocket nines on the button in a three-handed game, which is a very strong hand. I want to raise for value and to take control of the hand with position. A standard 3x open to 300 is appropriate here.
I am in the small blind with Ten-Eight offsuit facing a 3x raise from the button. This hand is too weak to play out of position against a pre-flop raise. Folding is the standard and correct decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a solid hand to defend against a Button open. Folding is too tight, and while a 3-bet is an option, calling is the standard, lower-variance play to see a flop with a reasonable hand in position-neutral terms, especially given the stack sizes and the current tournament stage.

The board is highly coordinated with a King and Jack, which hits the Button's opening range much harder than my range. Holding only a middle pair of nines with an Ace kicker, I am likely behind any Broadway hand or better pair. Checking is the standard, conservative play to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on the opponent's continuation bet or check.

I have flopped a set of nines on a board of Ks-Js-9d. This is an extremely strong hand, but the board is very wet with many straight and flush draws (any Ten or Queen makes a straight, and there are two spades). I need to bet for value and to protect my hand against these draws. Since Flashlight checked, I will make a continuation bet of about 60-70% of the pot to build the pot and charge drawing hands.

I have middle pair (nines) on a board that contains a King and a Jack. While this board is favorable for the button's opening range, a continuation bet of 450 into a 650 pot is a standard sizing that could be made with many hands, including air, draws, or weak Broadway pairs. Folding here is too weak given the pot odds and my pair, and raising turns my marginal hand into a bluff on a board that hits their range. I will call to evaluate the turn.

I have middle pair (nines) on a board with two broadway cards and a completed flush draw on the turn. Since I am out of position, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and act as a bluff catcher. Betting would likely only get called by better hands, and folding is premature as I still have some showdown value. I will check to see the opponent's action and re-evaluate.

The turn 7s is a highly coordinated card that completes a potential flush and maintains the straight possibility of any Queen-Ten. Although I have a set of nines, the board is now extremely dangerous. Having the 9 of spades provides some minimal protection, but most of Flashlight's calling range on the flop (straight draws with a spade, or two spades) has now improved. By checking behind, I control the pot size and give myself a chance to see a safe river or hit a full house if the board pairs. If Flashlight has a flush or straight, I've minimized my losses; if they are drawing, I'm not giving them an opportunity to check-raise me off my equity.

I am at the river with a pair of nines on a board of J-9-K-7-6. The opponent bet the flop but checked the turn, which indicates they likely have a marginal hand or were semi-bluffing a draw that missed. My pair of nines has some showdown value, but betting would likely only be called by better hands, as the opponent would fold most of the air I currently beat. Therefore, checking to induce a bluff or simply taking the showdown is the most prudent play.

I have a set of nines on a board with three spades and potential straight draws. Flashlight has checked on both the turn and the river after calling my flop bet. This passivity suggests they likely do not have a flush or a straight, as they would typically lead the river for value after I checked back the turn. I believe my set is the best hand against their likely range of one-pair or two-pair hands (like Kx or Jx). I will place a value bet of approximately half the pot to extract chips from those weaker made hands while maintaining the ability to fold if faced with a check-raise.

The opponent (Button) checked the turn, which significantly weakened their range. By betting the river after checking the turn, the opponent is polarized: they either hit a weak pair or are turning a missed draw (like missed spades or straight draws) into a bluff. My pair of nines acts as a strong bluff catcher in this spot. Given the pot odds and the high likelihood of a missed draw bluffing here, calling is the mathematically correct decision.
I am on the button with 6s 3s, which is a suited 3-gapper and generally too weak to open raise even from late position. Folding is the standard, most mathematically sound choice to preserve chips.

I am in the small blind with King-high suited, and the button (Pro) has folded. It is standard to complete the small blind and see a flop against the big blind when I am already invested for 50 chips. A call is a low-risk way to play a decent suited hand.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak hand (T2o). Flashlight limped from the Small Blind after Pro folded on the button. Since I have the option to see the flop for free, there is no reason to bluff-raise with such a low-equity hand. Checking is the standard and most prudent play.

I have flopped middle pair with a King kicker. The board is relatively dry with some spade potential. Checking allows me to control the pot size while out of position against the Big Blind. If Flash bets, I can re-evaluate, but for now, checking seems the most prudent play to see how they react.

I have flopped two pair (Tens and Deuces) in a limped pot from the Big Blind. This is a very strong hand in this scenario. Flashlight checked to me, and I need to bet for value and protection against potential draws, specifically the spade flush draw on the board. A bet of around 75% of the pot is appropriate to extract value from any Ten, Six, or draw that Flashlight might be holding.

I have flopped middle pair with a King kicker. My opponent (Flash) has bet 150 into a 100-chip pot, which is a small bet that suggests a probe or a marginal hand. Folding would be too conservative given my pair, and raising could force out hands that I am currently beating or isolate me against a stronger range. Calling is the most balanced approach, keeping the pot manageable and allowing me to re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold a pair of sixes on a 10-2-6-2 board. Since the turn has paired the board and my hand is a marginal made hand, checking is the most appropriate action. It allows me to control the pot size while out of position, preventing me from bloating the pot if I am behind, while also giving my opponent an opportunity to bluff if they are air. I will check to see the river or evaluate my opponent's action.

I have improved from two pair to a full house (deuces full of tens) on the turn. This is an exceptionally strong hand. Flashlight has checked for the second time, and I must continue to bet for value. Since the board paired the deuce, it is less likely Flashlight has a draw, but they could easily have a Ten, a Six, or a pocket pair. I will bet 350 into the 500 pot to build the pot and extract value from these weaker made hands while keeping the price reasonable enough for them to call.

I hold a pair of sixes on a 10-2-6-2 board. Flash's turn bet of 350 into a 400-chip pot suggests they might have a Ten, a draw, or are attempting to push me off a marginal hand. While the board pairing the deuce on the turn could potentially give my opponent trips, it also increases the likelihood of a bluff. My middle pair is a strong enough hand to continue as a bluff catcher, as folding here would be too passive against an aggressive opponent. I will call the 350 to see the river and re-evaluate.

The board is Ten, Two, Six, Two, Five. My hand is a pair of Sixes (Six of Hearts and King of Hearts) with a King kicker. The board is paired with Twos, making it quite dangerous; any Two gives the opponent trips, and any Ten or better pair beats me. Given the opponent's aggression on the flop and turn, they likely have a made hand or a draw. Betting would only isolate me against better hands, and checking allows me to control the pot and reach showdown cheaply. Since I have already checked in the log, I am committed to a check-call or check-fold depending on the opponent's action, but as the current decision point is effectively checking down the river, I will check.

I have a very strong hand, a full house (Deuces full of Tens), on a board of Ts-2s-6c-2c-5h. Flashlight limped from the Small Blind and has check-called my bets on the flop and turn. This suggests they have a made hand they are unwilling to fold, likely a Ten, a Six, or a pocket pair. While it is theoretically possible for them to have a better full house (like 66 or 55) or quads (with the 2h), these are statistically unlikely given the pre-flop action. My goal is to extract maximum value. In a previous hand (#40), a river bet of 800 was called by Flashlight, whereas a larger bet of 1500 in Hand #17 resulted in a fold. Therefore, I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot to maximize the likelihood of being called while still extracting significant value from weaker made hands like a Ten or a Six.

I have two pair, Sixes and Twos, with a King kicker. The board is T-2-6-2-5. I am losing to any 2, 5, or 6 that forms a better two-pair or trips, and any pocket pair of 5s, 6s, or 2s (full house/quads), but I am beating any single pair (like Tens or Aces) and any bluffs. Given the pot odds (calling 800 to win a total pot of 2800), I need to be right about 29% of the time. This is a classic bluff-catching situation where my hand is significantly stronger than a single pair, making it a mandatory call.

My starting hand, 9c 3c, is very weak and lacks high-card strength or significant playability. As the Button in a three-handed game, I should be disciplined and not enter the pot with such marginal cards. Folding allows me to preserve my stack of 7550 and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

I am holding 9h 2d in the small blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Since Flashlight has already folded, it is just me and the Big Blind. While I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop, playing 9-2 offsuit out of position against the Big Blind is generally a losing play. I have a healthy chip lead and there is no need to play trash hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. While a button open can be wide in a three-handed game, this hand is significantly below the standard opening range. Given the stack sizes and the tendency of the blinds to call or occasionally re-raise, there is no need to play such a weak hand from this position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K3o from the small blind is a weak, disconnected hand. Since I will be out of position against the Big Blind if they call, and my hand lacks significant playability or high card strength, folding is the most standard and solid play to preserve my stack.

62s is a very weak starting hand, even on the button in a 3-handed game. It has little playability post-flop, low equity, and is heavily dominated. The best and most standard play here is to simply fold and wait for a better spot to enter the pot.

I am in the small blind, and the button (Pro) has folded. This is a blind-vs-blind situation, and I hold Queen-Nine suited, which is a strong hand to play aggressively in this spot. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or define the range of the big blind.

I have Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Flashlight has raised from the small blind, which could be a wide range. I want to 3-bet for value and to take control of the hand, especially since I'll be out of position post-flop. A raise to 1,000 is a standard sizing that puts pressure on the small blind's opening range.

The opponent has 3-bet from the big blind in a blind-versus-blind scenario. While I am out of position, Queen-Nine suited is a playable hand that retains equity against a wide range. A call allows me to see a flop with a reasonable price, as folding would surrender my initial raise too easily against a potentially aggressive opponent. I will call the additional 700 to proceed.

The flop has come 2c-3c-7h, which completely misses my Queen-high hand. Given that I called a pre-flop 3-bet from the big blind, checking is the standard play to navigate this out-of-position situation. I have no pair and very little equity, so I will check to see how the opponent proceeds before deciding on any further action.

On a low board of 2c-3c-7h, I have Ace-King high with the nut flush draw (Ac). I 3-bet pre-flop, and my opponent checked to me on the flop. A continuation bet is standard here to maintain the initiative and pressure their medium-strength hands or non-pairs. If I am called, I still have two overcards and a strong flush draw for equity.

I am facing a 1000-chip bet into a 2000-chip pot on a board of 2c-3c-7h. As the pre-flop 3-better, Flash is representing significant strength. My hand, Queen-Nine high, has completely missed this flop and possesses almost no equity. Calling here would require putting in more chips with a weak range while out of position against a player who has shown aggression. Preserving my 6600-chip stack for a better situation is the disciplined strategic choice.

I am on the button with 9d-Ts. While this is a marginal hand, it is playable in late position. Given my strategy of aggressive button play and the need to accumulate chips, I will open-raise to steal the blinds.

I have been dealt Ace-King suited (hearts) in the Small Blind, which is a premium starting hand. Flashlight opened from the Button, and I should 3-bet both for value and to isolate. A raise to 1000 is consistent with my previous sizing in a similar situation and exerts significant pressure while building a pot with a very strong hand.
I am facing a button open and a small blind 3-bet. My hand, 93s, is far too weak to consider defending or 4-betting in this spot. The only reasonable action is to fold.

The opponent has 3-bet to 1000, which is a significant re-raise. Holding 9-10 offsuit, I am likely behind their 3-betting range, as this hand is dominated by the Broadway cards and higher pairs typically found in a 3-betting range. While I have the advantage of position, the strength of my hand does not justify calling such a large raise in this situation. Folding is the disciplined move to preserve my stack for a more favorable opportunity.

I have been dealt pocket Aces (Ad Ac) on the Button, the best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With a stack of 14,600 and the blinds at 50/100, I want to build a pot immediately. My standard opening raise has been 300 chips, and I will maintain that sizing to remain balanced while ensuring I extract value from my premium holding.

K4 suited is a weak hand to play from the small blind facing a 3x open from the button. Playing out of position with a marginal holding and reverse implied odds is not profitable, especially with the big blind still to act. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Q8o against a button open from an aggressive opponent (Flash). Given the pot odds of 200 to call for a potential pot of 650, defending with this hand is a standard play to see a flop. I will call to proceed.

The flop of 3h-4s-3d is very dry and offers no help to my Queen-high hand.
